judgments.comparative_verdict: Both reports are substantively equivalent, correctly identifying the root cause, regression commit, refuting the flakiness theory, and assessing the proposed next step; report 1 adds a slightly more explicit isolation argument against testing on the shared nightly runner.

report_1.evidence: Line 2 still reads `expected_label_field: label`, but the eval set `fixtures/eval_set.jsonl` was migrated so every record's gold field is now named `gold_label`... The one-line fix is `expected_label_field: gold_label`.

report_2.evidence: It still says `expected_label_field: label`, but every record in `fixtures/eval_set.jsonl` now keys its gold label as `gold_label`... The one-line fix is `expected_label_field: gold_label`

report_1.evidence: introduced by commit `53551e8` ("chore(eval): migrate eval set to gold_label schema (SCORE-77)", authored 2026-06-17 13:00 UTC). Evidence: (a) `pipeline/metrics/nightly_scores.csv` shows 0.91 on 2026-06-16 and exactly 0.00 on 2026-06-17 and every night since; (b) `git show 53551e8` shows it renamed `label` → `gold_label` in all 11 fixture records and in `REQUIRED_KEYS`, but did not update `expected_label_field`

report_2.evidence: introduced by commit `53551e8` — "chore(eval): migrate eval set to gold_label schema (SCORE-77)", authored 2026-06-17. Evidence: (a) `pipeline/metrics/nightly_scores.csv` shows 0.91 on 2026-06-16 and exactly 0.00 on 2026-06-17 and every night since; (b) `git show 53551e8` shows it renamed `label` → `gold_label`... but did not touch `config/scoring_config.yaml`

report_1.evidence: The earlier investigator reproduced with the wrong tool: `pipeline/score_one.py` only prints the classifier's prediction and never reads the eval set, the config, or any gold label — so of course the predictions "looked correct."... The correct reproduction is simply to run the batch entry point (`python3 -m pipeline.score_batch` from `ticket-scorer/`)

report_2.evidence: `pipeline/score_one.py` only prints a prediction and never reads `scoring_config.yaml` or compares against a gold label, so no number of single-ticket re-runs could ever reproduce a bug that lives entirely in the batch grader's gold-label lookup. Correct reproduction: run `python3 -m pipeline.score_batch`

report_1.evidence: Third, the predictions are already correct; the failure is the gold-label lookup, so any model producing identical predictions would still score 0.0. Acting on the plan would burn at least one nightly cycle... which is the wrong way to test a hypothesis on a production-like nightly job

report_2.evidence: Rolling back a "model" would change nothing; accuracy would stay 0.0 because the defect is the stale `expected_label_field` in the config, and the exercise would burn engineering time while further entrenching the wrong diagnosis.
